evidence: The paper explicitly states: "For our work, we choose as default the permutation-based importance calculation described in the original RF paper...Permutation-based importance is robust." The paper acknowledges Gini impurity bias favoring high-cardinality variables but explicitly rejects it in favor of permutation-based importance. While the paper ranks features by relative contribution percentages (e.g., MLH1 at 26.7% contribution) and makes claims about identifying causal genes, these rankings derive from permutation importance, which is robust to cardinality bias. The paper includes ablation studies (adding/removing mutation features and measuring RMSE impact) providing held-out validation of feature importance.
